Nor invoke besides Allah that which neither benefits you nor can do you any harm. For if you do so, then you will indeed be among the wrongdoers.’’ ’ (106)

Key Points

"Hanīf" (حَنیف) means straight and upright, in which there is no deviation whatsoever to the left or the right.

Messages

1- Piety requires resolve, determination, and love. «اقم وجهك»

2- The religion of Islam is a path without deviation. «حنیفاً»

3- A true Muslim, after accepting Islam, does not become tainted by any polytheism (shirk) and does not incline toward the tyrants (taghuts). «ولاتكوننّ من المشركین»

4- The wise either work for profit or to avert danger and repel harm. Idols neither provide benefit nor possess the power to repel harm. Therefore, polytheism (shirk) is a type of foolishness. «لا تدع... ما لاینفعك و لایضرّك»

5- Polytheism (shirk) is oppression (dhulm), and the polytheist (mushrik) is an oppressor (dhālim). «فانّك اذا من الظالمین»

Ayah

وَإِن يَمْسَسْكَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ فَلَا كَاشِفَ لَهُۥٓ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ وَإِن يُرِدْكَ بِخَيْرٍ فَلَا رَآدَّ لِفَضْلِهِۦ ۚ يُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (۱٠۷)
Should Allah visit you with some distress, there is no one to remove it except Him; and should He desire any good for you, none can stand in the way of His grace: He grants it to whomever He wishes of His servants, and He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (107)

Key Points

The fact that the expression "الاّ هو" (except Him) is used in removing harm, but not in removing favor, is because Allah removes the harm that reaches a person, but He does not remove the favor that reaches a person, unless our own actions change it. [369] As stated in the Quran: «انّ اللَّه لایغیّر ما بقومٍ حتى یغیّروا ما بأنفسهم» [370] meaning the change of blessing depends on the performance of the person himself. [371]

369) Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani. 370) Ar-Ra'd, 11. 371) In the Kumayl Supplication, we also read: «اللّهم اغفر لى الذنوب الّتى تغیّر النعم»

Messages

1- Good and evil are solely in the hand of Allah. «ان یمسسك اللّه بضرّ، یردك بخیر»

2- Not every bitterness or harm is evil (shar). Sometimes bitter events are for awakening the innate nature (fitrah) or for testing man. «بُضّرٍ» (with harm) and not «بشرٍّ» (with evil).

3- Allah's intention is to bestow good. Therefore, regarding good, the term «یردك» (He intends for you) and intention (iradah) are used, while regarding harm, the expression «یمسسك» (He touches you) is used. [372]

4- The good that reaches man is Divine favor (fadl), not human entitlement. «لفضله»

5- Allah's Will is based on wisdom, and His decrees (mashiyyat) are understood alongside His being the All-Wise (Hakim). «یصیب به من یشاء»

372) Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani.

Ayah

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ (۱٠۸)
Say, ‘O mankind! The truth has already come to you from your Lord. Whoever is guided, is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment, and it is not my business to watch over you.’ (108)

Messages

1- Whatever has come from Allah (such as the Quran and the teachings of the prophets) is all truth and pertains to the affairs of His Lordship. «الحقّ من ربّكم»

2- The goal of the mission of the prophets and the sending down of the heavenly books is the nurturing of all mankind. «یا ایها الناس... ربّكم»

3- Allah has completed the proof (Hujjah), and we have no excuse for rejecting the truth. «قد جاءكم الحقّ»

4- Allah is independent of our guidance; the benefit and harm of guidance and misguidance return only to ourselves. «لنفسه»

5- Man possesses free will, and the destiny of every person is in his own hands; even the prophets do not have the right to compel people. «ما انا علیكم بوكیلٍ»

6- The duty of the prophets is guidance and propagation, not compulsion or coercion. «ما أنا علیكم بوكیل»

Ayah

وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَٱصْبِرْ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (۱٠۹)
And follow that which is revealed to you, and be patient until Allah issues [His] judgement, and He is the best of judges. (109)

Messages

1- Whether people believe or disbelieve, we must not separate ourselves from divine revelation and commands. «واتبع...»

2- The Prophets are submissive to the command of revelation. «واتبع ما یوحى»

3- The descent of revelation has been gradual. «یوحى» (The imperfect verb indicates continuity.)

4- Following revelation requires patience. «واتّبع... اصبر»

5- The leader must be patient. «واتّبع ما یوحى الیك و اصبر»

6- Knowledge is a prerequisite for action, «واتّبع ما یوحى» and steadfastness is a prerequisite for victory. «واصبر حتى یحكم اللَّه»

7- We should not worry about the future, for Allah is the Best of Judges. «خیر الحاكمین»

«اَلحَمدُللَّه ربّ العالمین»

Portrait of Surah Hud

This Surah was revealed in the final years of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله presence in Mecca, years during which the Muslims were living in extremely difficult circumstances due to the passing of Hadrat Abu Talib and Hadrat Khadijah.

The Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Surah Hud made me old. [373] This is because the verse of steadfastness was revealed in this Surah: «فاستقم كما امرت و من تاب معك» [374] (Be steadfast as you have been commanded, and those who have repented with you.) Just as you have been commanded, be firm and patient, and your companions must also be firm. However, the impatience of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله companions distressed him.

The content of this Surah, which has 123 verses, is the history of the Prophets, especially Hadrat Nuh عليه السلام, and attention to matters of belief. Careful study of the history of the Prophets shows that the movement of the Prophets is a continuous historical process, not a fleeting incident. The history of the Prophets and their nations is governed by laws, and studying it clarifies the secret of the downfall or honor of nations, demonstrating that religion is not separate from society and that the destiny of a society is entirely dependent on the religion and creed of its people.

The name of Hadrat Hud عليه السلام is mentioned five times in this Surah, which is equal to the repetition of this name in the rest of the Quran.

374) Hud, 112.
